does: This paper proposes targeted latent adversarial training (LAT) to improve LLM robustness against jailbreaking and harmful behavior. Rather than using untargeted latent space attacks that generically maximize loss, the method employs adversarial examples that minimize loss on specific competing tasks representing undesirable behaviors. The authors experiment with this targeted approach as an augmentation to various state-of-the-art safety fine-tuning methods, demonstrating that incorporating adversarial perturbations during training on latent activations can help models maintain harmless behavior even under targeted attack. The core contribution is showing that targeting specific failure modes during adversarial training provides better robustness than generic perturbation-based approaches, suggesting that understanding and directly contending with particular harmful capabilities yields more effective defenses.
connects: This work builds on recent insights from red-teaming, model editing, and interpretability research indicating that standard fine-tuning suppresses rather than eliminates harmful capabilities in LLMs. It extends prior work on latent adversarial training, which has been explored in other domains to improve model robustness, by introducing a targeted variant that addresses specific failure modes rather than generic vulnerabilities. The paper responds to the jailbreaking literature, which has demonstrated numerous techniques for eliciting harmful outputs from supposedly aligned models, while drawing on interpretability findings that suggest undesirable behaviors persist in latent representations. By combining these threads, the work connects adversarial training methodology with the specific safety challenges of LLMs and the assumption that robustness requires addressing competing objectives embedded in model representations.
enables: This approach could substantially improve the robustness of deployed LLMs against adversarial attacks and jailbreaking attempts by more directly targeting the competing objectives that generate harmful behaviors. It points toward a paradigm where safety training explicitly models and defends against specific failure modes rather than broadly suppressing capabilities. Future work could explore automated methods for identifying and prioritizing which competing tasks to target, investigate whether targeted LAT helps remove capabilities more completely rather than just suppressing them, and examine transferability of robustness across different types of harmful behaviors. The method also suggests that interpretability work identifying which latent features correspond to harmful behaviors could further strengthen defenses. More broadly, it opens directions for adversarial training approaches that leverage detailed knowledge of failure modes to create more fundamentally aligned models.
